title: Fraction multiplication as scaling
description: Interpret how multiplying by a fraction greater or less than 1 affects the product.
topic_path: Module 4: Multiplication and division of fractions and decimal fractions
Topic F: Multiplication with fractions and decimals as scaling and word problems

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare using $>,<,$ or $=$.**  
*Hint: Try to solve without multiplying.*

$\dfrac14 \times5{,}000~$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $5{,}000$
hints:
How does $\purpleE{{\dfrac14}\times 5{,}000}$ compare to $5{,}000$ on a number line?

[[☃ image 1]]

Since $\purpleE{\dfrac14}<1$, multiplying $5{,}000$ by $\purpleE{\dfrac14}$ will scale $5{,}000$ down to a smaller number.
${{\dfrac14}\times 5{,}000} < 5{,}000$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare using $>,<,$ or $=$.**  
*Hint: Try to solve without multiplying.*

$60\times\dfrac23~$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $60$
hints:
How does $\purpleE{60\times\dfrac23}$ compare to $60$ on a number line?

[[☃ image 2]]


Since $\purpleE{\dfrac23}<1$, multiplying $60$ by $\purpleE{\dfrac23}$ will scale $60$ down to a smaller number.
$60\times\dfrac23 < 60$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare using $>,<,$ or $=$.**  
*Hint: Try to solve without multiplying.*

$15$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac25\times15$
hints:
How does $15$ compare to $\purpleE{\dfrac25\times15}$ on a number line?

[[☃ image 1]]

Since $\purpleE{\dfrac25}<1$, multiplying $15$ by $\purpleE{\dfrac25}$ will scale $15$ down to a smaller number.
$15 > \dfrac25\times15$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare using $>,<,$ or $=$.**  
*Hint: Try to solve without multiplying.*

$20$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $20\times\dfrac65$
hints:
How does $20$ compare to $\purpleE{20\times\dfrac65}$ on a number line?

[[☃ explanation 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

Since $\purpleE{\dfrac65}>1$, multiplying $20$ by $\purpleE{\dfrac65}$ will scale $20$ up to a larger number.
$20<20\times\dfrac65$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare using $>,<,$ or $=$.**  
*Hint: Try to solve without multiplying.*

$\dfrac77 \times36~$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $36$
hints: Let's remember that $\purpleE{\dfrac77}=\blueE1$.

Therefore:

$\begin{align}
\purpleE{\dfrac77}\times36&=\blueE1\times36\\\\
&=36
\end{align}$
$\dfrac77\times36=36$ because we are multiplying $36$ by a fraction that is *equal to* $1$.
$\dfrac77\times36=36$

assessment_items.question_content:
**How will the product of $7\times\dfrac54$  compare to $7$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
How does $\purpleE{7 \times \dfrac54}$ compare to $7$ on a number line?

[[☃ explanation 1]]


[[☃ image 1]]
Since $\purpleE{\dfrac54}>1$, multiplying $7$ by $\purpleE{\dfrac54}$ will scale $7$ up to a larger number.
$7\times\dfrac54>7$
$7\times\dfrac54$ will be greater than $7$ because we are multiplying $7$ by a fraction that is *greater than* $1$.

choices.content: It will be less because we are multiplying $7$ by a fraction that is *less than* $1$.
It will be equal because we are multiplying $7$ by a fraction that is *equal to* $1$.
It will be greater because we are multiplying $7$ by a fraction that is *greater than* $1$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How will the product of $72\times\dfrac37$  compare to $72$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
How does $\purpleE{72 \times \dfrac37}$ compare to $72$ on a number line? 

[[☃ image 1]]
Since $\purpleE{\dfrac37}<1$, multiplying $72$ by $\purpleE{\dfrac37}$ will scale $72$ down to a smaller number.
$72\times\dfrac37 < 72$
$72\times\dfrac37$ will be less than $72$ because we are multiplying $72$ by a fraction that is *less than* $1$.

choices.content: It will be less because we are multiplying $72$ by a fraction that is *less than* $1$.
It will be equal because we are multiplying $72$ by a fraction that is *equal to* $1$.
It will be greater because we are multiplying $72$ by a fraction that is *greater than* $1$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How will the product of $16\times\dfrac33$  compare to $16$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We know that $\purpleE{\dfrac33}=\blueE1$.

Therefore:

$\begin{align}
16\times\purpleE{\dfrac33}&=16\times\blueE1\\\\
&=16
\end{align}$
$16\times\dfrac33 =16$
$16\times\dfrac33$ will be equal to $16$ because we are multiplying $16$ by a fraction that is *equal to* $1$.

choices.content: It will be less because we are multiplying $16$ by a fraction that is *less than* $1$.
It will be equal because we are multiplying $16$ by a fraction that is *equal to* $1$.
It will be greater because we are multiplying $16$ by a fraction that is *greater than* $1$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How will the product of $\dfrac75\times 15$  compare to $15$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
How does $\purpleE{\dfrac75 \times 15}$ compare to $15$ on a number line? 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]
Since $\purpleE{\dfrac75}>1$, multiplying $15$ by $\purpleD{\dfrac75}$ will scale $15$ up to a larger number.
$\dfrac75 \times 15 > 15$
$\dfrac75 \times 15$ will be greater than $15$ because we are multiplying $15$ by a fraction that is *greater than* $1$.

choices.content: It will be less because we are multiplying $15$ by a fraction that is *less than* $1$.
It will be equal because we are multiplying $15$ by a fraction that is *equal to* $1$.
It will be greater because we are multiplying $15$ by a fraction that is *greater than* $1$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How will the product of $45$  compare to $45\times\dfrac{250}{250}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We know that $\purpleE{\dfrac{250}{250}}=\blueE1$.

Therefore:

$\begin{align}
45\times\purpleE{\dfrac{250}{250}}&=45\times\blueE1\\\\
&=45
\end{align}$
$45\times\dfrac{250}{250}$ will be equal to $45$ because we are multiplying $45$ by a fraction that is *equal to* $1$.

choices.content: It will be less because we are multiplying $45$ by a fraction that is *less than* $1$.
It will be equal because we are multiplying $45$ by a fraction that is *equal to* $1$.
It will be greater because we are multiplying $45$ by a fraction that is *greater than* $1$.

hints:
If we multiply $17$ by $\blueE{\text{any number greater than } 1}$, $17$ will be scaled up to a number that is greater than $17$.

Which expressions multiply $17$ by a$\blueE{\text{ number greater than } 1}?$

$17 \times {\dfrac3{8}}$ :

${\dfrac38}<1$, so $17$ will be scaled down to a number less than $17$.

$17 \times {\dfrac1{4}}$ :

${\dfrac14}<1$, so $17$ will be scaled down to a number less than $17$.

$17 \times \dfrac9{8} $ :

$\blueE{\dfrac9{8} > 1}$, so $17$ will be scaled up to a number greater than $17$.

$17 \times \dfrac{12}{8} $ :

$\blueE{\dfrac{12}{8} > 1}$, so $17$ will be scaled up to a number greater than $17$.

The following expressions are greater than $17$ :

* $17 \times \dfrac9{8} $

* $17 \times \dfrac{12}{8}$

If we multiply $95$ by $\blueE{\text{any number less than } 1}$, $95$ will be scaled down to a number that is less than $95$.

Which expressions multiply $95$ by a$\blueE{\text{ number less than } 1}?$

$95 \times {\dfrac5{4}}$ :

${\dfrac54}>1$, so $95$ will be scaled up to a number greater than $95$.

$95 \times {\dfrac4{7}}$ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac47}<1}$, so $95$ will be scaled down to a number less than $95$.

$95 \times {\dfrac12}$ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac12}<1}$, so $95$ will be scaled down to a number less than $95$.

$95 \times {\dfrac88}$ :

${\dfrac88}=1$, so $95$ will remain the same.

The following expressions are less than $95$ :

* $95 \times {\dfrac47}$

* $95\times\dfrac12$

If we multiply $148$ by $\blueE{\text{any number equal to } 1}$, $148$ will remain the same.

Which expressions multiply $148$ by a$\blueE{\text{ number equal to } 1}?$

$148 \times {\dfrac33}$ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac33}=1}$, so $148$ will remain the same.

$148 \times {\dfrac89}$ :

${{\dfrac89}<1}$, so $148$ will be scaled down to a number less than $148$.

$148 \times {\dfrac32}$ :

${{\dfrac32}>1}$, so $148$ will be scaled up to a number greater than $148$.

$148 \times {\dfrac{25}{25}}$ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac{25}{25}}=1}$, so $148$ will remain the same.

The following expressions are equal to $148$ :

* $148 \times {\dfrac33}$

* $148 \times {\dfrac{25}{25}}$

If we multiply $915$ by $\blueE{\text{any number equal to } 1}$, $915$ will remain the same.

Which expressions multiply $915$ by a$\blueE{\text{ number equal to } 1}?$

$915 \times {\dfrac53}$ :

${{\dfrac53}>1}$, so $915$ will be scaled up to a number greater than $915$.

$915 \times \dfrac{100}{100} $ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac{100}{100}}=1}$, so $915$ will remain the same.

$915 \times \dfrac22 $ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac22}=1}$, so $915$ will remain the same.

$915 \times \dfrac{10}{100} $ :

$\dfrac{10}{100}<1$, so $915$ will be scaled down to a number less than $915$.

The following expressions are equal to $915$ :

* $915 \times \dfrac{100}{100} $

* $915 \times \dfrac22$

If we multiply $610$ by $\blueE{\text{any number less than } 1}$, $610$ will be scaled down to a number that is less than $610$.

Which expressions multiply $610$ by a$\blueE{\text{ number less than } 1}?$

$610 \times \dfrac5{4} $ :

${{\dfrac54}>1}$, so $610$ will be scaled up to a number greater than than $610$.

$610 \times \dfrac2{3} $ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac23}<1}$, so $610$ will be scaled down to a number less than $610$.

$610 \times \dfrac32$ :

${{\dfrac32}>1}$, so $610$ will be scaled up to a number greater than than $610$.

$610 \times \dfrac5{7} $ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac57}<1}$, so $610$ will be scaled down to a number less than $610$.

The following expressions are less than $610$ :

* $610 \times \dfrac2{3} $ 

* $610 \times \dfrac5{7} $

If we multiply $250$ by $\blueE{\text{any number greater than } 1}$, $250$ will be scaled up to a number that is greater than $250$.

Which expressions multiply $250$ by a$\blueE{\text{ number greater than } 1}?$

$\dfrac1{6} \times 250 $ :

${{\dfrac16}<1}$, so $250$ will be scaled down to a number less than $250$.

$\dfrac9{8} \times 250 $ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac98}>1}$, so $250$ will be scaled up to a number greater than $250$.

$\dfrac9{10} \times 250 $ :

${{\dfrac9{10}}<1}$, so $250$ will be scaled down to a number less than $250$.

$2\times 250 $ :

$\blueE{2>1}$, so $250$ will be scaled up to a number greater than $250$.

The following expressions are greater than $250$ :

* $\dfrac9{8} \times 250 $

* $2 \times 250 $

If we multiply $\dfrac56$ by $\blueE{\text{any number greater than } 1}$, $\dfrac56$ will be scaled up to a number that is greater than $\dfrac56$ .

Which expressions multiply $\dfrac56$ by a$\blueE{\text{ number greater than } 1}?$

$\dfrac56 \times \dfrac2{2} $ :

${{\dfrac22}=1}$, so $\dfrac56 $ will remain the same.

$\dfrac56 \times 1\dfrac1{3} $ :

$\blueE{{1\dfrac13}>1}$, so $\dfrac56$ will be scaled up to a number greater than $\dfrac56$ .

$\dfrac56 \times \dfrac68$ :

${{\dfrac68}<1}$, so $\dfrac56$ will be scaled down to a number less than $\dfrac56$.

$\dfrac56 \times \dfrac7{2} $ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac{7}{2}}>1}$, so $\dfrac56$ will be scaled up to a number greater than $\dfrac56$ .

The following expressions are greater than $\dfrac56$ :

* $\dfrac56 \times 1\dfrac1{3} $ 

* $\dfrac56 \times \dfrac7{2} $

If we multiply $\dfrac78$ by $\blueE{\text{any number less than } 1}$, $\dfrac78$ will be scaled down to a number that is less than $\dfrac78$.

Which expressions multiply $\dfrac78$ by a$\blueE{\text{ number less than } 1}?$

$\dfrac78 \times \dfrac9{15} $ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac9{15}}<1}$, so $\dfrac78$ will be scaled down to a number less than $\dfrac78$ .

$\dfrac78 \times \dfrac{3}{4} $ :

$\blueE{{\dfrac34}<1}$, so $\dfrac78$ will be scaled down to a number less than $\dfrac78$ .

$\dfrac78 \times \dfrac99$ :

${{\dfrac99}=1}$, so $\dfrac78$ will stay the same.

$\dfrac78 \times \dfrac43$ :

${{\dfrac43}>1}$, so $\dfrac78$ will be scaled up to a number greater than $\dfrac78$ .

The following expressions are less than $\dfrac78$ :

* $\dfrac78 \times \dfrac9{15} $ 

* $\dfrac78 \times \dfrac{3}{4} $
